title: Episode 2

jokes.text: Priest says 'And also with you' in response to a sung liturgical response — breaking the ritual exchange at the wrong moment
jokes.setup: The entire 'Peace be with you / And also with you' liturgical call-and-response has been performed correctly and solemnly for a full minute
jokes.punchline: The Priest reflexively fires back 'And also with you' to the choir's sung line, suggesting he's so auto-piloted through the ritual he can't even stop himself
jokes.explanation: A subtle but well-observed bit of character comedy — the Priest breaking his own ritual through sheer conditioned reflex. It rewards attentive viewers and establishes the character's self-aware awkwardness without announcing it. Economy is its strength: zero extra words needed.

jokes.text: Priest ends his notices with 'That's all, folks' — a Looney Tunes sign-off during a solemn mass
jokes.setup: The Priest has been delivering perfectly ordinary parish notices — football results, sick calls, communion prep — in an awkward, stumbling manner
jokes.punchline: 'That's all, folks' lands as a cartoonishly casual sign-off to a church congregation, instantly deflating the sacred register
jokes.explanation: The Looney Tunes reference is an inspired bit of character-specific incongruity. It's maximally economical — three words do all the work — and it's completely character-specific: this is exactly the kind of dorky, unconscious pop-culture slip a bookish, slightly awkward priest would make. Earns a high craft score for character integration and economy. The delivery in context of everything preceding it lands with genuine surprise.

jokes.text: 'I thought you'd be in prison by now.' / 'Oh, well, I keep trying, but they just won't have me.'
jokes.setup: The Priest greets Fleabag warmly after the service in a casual post-mass exchange
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's cheerful admission that she's actively trying to get arrested but failing is a perfectly calibrated self-deprecating absurdist comeback
jokes.explanation: This exchange does enormous character work in seconds. The Priest's opening line establishes he knows exactly who Fleabag is and isn't scandalized — he's dry and warm simultaneously. Fleabag's response is the real gem: it's self-deprecating, absurdist, confessional, and darkly funny all at once, and it's completely her voice. The 'but they just won't have me' construction is the key — it implies ongoing effort and repeated failure, which is funnier and sadder than a simple 'I'm trying.' High marks across the board. Justifies 8+ because this exchange is memorable, character-specific, and would land in any context without explanation.

jokes.text: 'I'm sorry about your eye.' / 'Oh, that's OK. Gives me some edge. I've told them some heroic bullshit.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag apologizes about the Priest's black eye — which we understand was caused by her punching her brother-in-law Martin at dinner in Episode 1
jokes.punchline: The Priest reveals he's been lying to his congregation about how he got the eye, calling his own lies 'heroic bullshit' with cheerful nonchalance
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'heroic bullshit' is a perfectly specific construction — not 'lies' or 'stories' — and the casual self-awareness of a priest admitting he's been spinning tales to his flock about a black eye he got in a chaotic dinner brawl is rich. Character integration is high because this detail fleshes out a man who is both holy and comfortably human.

jokes.text: Fleabag trying to pay the Priest back in 'instalments' while he insists he has no pockets
jokes.setup: Fleabag attempts to hand money to the Priest to repay him for dinner; he keeps refusing
jokes.punchline: 'I've got no pockets' — the cassock has no pockets, so there's literally nowhere for the money to go, which is both practically true and a perfect comic deflection
jokes.explanation: The pocketless cassock is a genuine and specific physical detail that most writers wouldn't think to use. It works on two levels: practical absurdity (he literally cannot take the money) and as a metaphor for a man who has renounced material attachment. Solid, specific comedy.

jokes.text: Fleabag turns to camera: 'That's Pam.' — after Pam announces she's going to 'knife the candles'
jokes.setup: Pam, the church helper, interrupts to announce she's going to 'knife the candles' — an oddly violent-sounding phrasing for a mundane task
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's direct-to-camera aside 'That's Pam' delivers the maximum information with minimum words — an entire character sketch compressed into two words
jokes.explanation: This is a masterclass in Fleabag's fourth-wall-breaking mode. 'That's Pam' works because it implies everything without stating anything: Pam is clearly a known quantity, a force of chaos that Fleabag has learned to simply acknowledge and move past. The economy here is extraordinary — two words create a full supporting character portrait. The character integration is perfect because this is quintessentially how Fleabag communicates with us: sharing her inner world through the most minimal possible utterance. Justifies 8+ comfortably.

jokes.text: 'Oh, is that holy?' / 'A bit less than it was before.'
jokes.setup: Water has spilled on what appears to be something sacred — holy water, a sacred cloth, a Bible — and Fleabag asks if the soaked item is holy
jokes.punchline: 'A bit less than it was before' — the Priest's casual acknowledgment that holiness has been somewhat diminished by the spill is a beautiful piece of deadpan theology
jokes.explanation: This is an exceptional joke. 'A bit less than it was before' treats holiness as a measurable, depletable quality — like a battery level — which is both theologically absurd and weirdly logical. The Priest delivers it with utter calm, as if this is a perfectly reasonable thing to say. It reveals a man deeply comfortable with the material reality of sacred objects while maintaining belief in their significance. The economy is flawless: every word earns its place. This is the kind of line that gets quoted. Justifies 8+ on every dimension.

jokes.text: 'He'll understand. He's an understanding sort.'
jokes.setup: Something sacred has been damaged or defiled
jokes.punchline: The Priest casually refers to God with the same mild, everyday language you'd use about a reasonable neighbor — 'He's an understanding sort' — reducing the divine to a genial acquaintance
jokes.explanation: Perfectly calibrated character comedy. The Priest's familiarity with God is rendered with such casual intimacy that it crosses from faith into something almost like a working relationship with a tolerant supervisor. 'An understanding sort' is the specific phrase that elevates this — it's the language of a mild English understatement applied to the Almighty. Character-specific, economical, and quietly profound.

jokes.text: The Priest offers Fleabag cans of G&T from M&S in a church sacristy
jokes.setup: They're in the vestry/back room of a church surrounded by religious paraphernalia, after a spill on holy objects
jokes.punchline: The Priest produces pre-mixed M&S gin-and-tonics in cans as the natural follow-up to the chaos, treating it as the obvious refreshment of choice
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'M&S' is key — not just gin, but the particular British bourgeois comfort of Marks & Spencer canned G&T in a church vestry. It characterizes the Priest as someone who manages his spiritual and human life with the same unfussy practicality. Competent, charming character work.

jokes.text: 'So, you're a cool priest, are you?' / 'No, I'm a big reader with no friends. Are you a cool person?' / 'Well, I don't believe in God.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag opens with a slightly teasing question about whether he's a 'cool priest' — the trope of the hip, relatable clergy
jokes.punchline: The Priest deflects the compliment with a self-deprecating 'big reader with no friends,' then inverts the question back at Fleabag, whose own definition of 'normal' turns out to be 'I don't believe in God' — which in this context is its own kind of confession
jokes.explanation: The exchange works because both halves land. The Priest's 'big reader with no friends' is honest and oddly charming — he's not performing humility, he's just accurate. But Fleabag's punch — defining herself as 'normal' by atheism to a priest — is the real joke, and it's structurally perfect: she asked him a defining question, he turned it back, and her answer reveals everything about her while being simultaneously funny and vulnerable.

jokes.text: Fleabag's fourth-wall aside: 'I love it when He does that.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has just said 'I don't believe in God' to a priest — the moment should be awkward or fraught
jokes.punchline: Fleabag turns to camera with 'I love it when He does that' — crediting God with engineering the moment, which is both a joke about the scene and an involuntary theological concession from an atheist
jokes.explanation: This is the standout joke of the episode and one of the finest moments in the series. The layers are extraordinary: (1) Fleabag the atheist involuntarily credits God with a perfect dramatic irony; (2) she's sharing this with the audience, not the Priest, which makes it conspiratorial and intimate; (3) it retroactively frames the entire scene as divinely staged; (4) it reveals that she is already half in love with the situation and possibly the man. 'I love it when He does that' treats God as a writer who just delivered the perfect beat — which is meta, theological, romantic, and funny simultaneously. Five words. Exceptional in every dimension. Among the best jokes in the entire series.

jokes.text: 'Is the father alright?' / 'Well, he's... he doesn't really... exist.'
jokes.setup: The Priest is offering condolences about Fleabag's miscarriage. Asking after 'the father' seems like natural pastoral care.
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's halting, euphemistic 'he doesn't really... exist' is ambiguous — it could mean he's absent, it could mean she doesn't know who he is, it could mean something darker. The Priest accepts it with gentle understanding — and the joke is that 'I understand' is probably also doing double theological duty (as in, God understands absent fathers).
jokes.explanation: Dark, layered, and smartly constructed. The misdirection works because 'doesn't really exist' sounds like it should be clarified but never is — leaving it beautifully suspended between meanings. The Priest's 'I understand' is the second layer: he means it pastorally, but it also reads as God understanding absent fathers, which is an inadvertent theological joke. Strong craft.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, I gathered that by the smelling of the Bible.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has insisted she is genuinely an atheist after the Priest offered theological comfort
jokes.punchline: The Priest gently reveals he already knew — because she sniffed the Bible rather than reading it, a detail he noticed and filed away — delivered with total dry calm
jokes.explanation: Exceptional line. 'The smelling of the Bible' is a callback to something we saw (or are told we saw) — the specific physical detail of Fleabag sniffing a Bible is both endearingly weird and completely in character. The Priest's phrasing 'I gathered that by the smelling of the Bible' has the cadence and formality of liturgy — 'by the smelling of the Bible' sounds like a clause from a prayer — which makes it funnier. It also establishes his quiet, perceptive attention to her. High scores across the board; justifies 8+.

jokes.text: The Priest writes restaurant reviews for the parish magazine
jokes.setup: Fleabag notices the Priest is working on something and asks about it
jokes.punchline: It's restaurant reviews — for the parish magazine — an absurdly mundane pursuit for a priest, done with apparent seriousness
jokes.explanation: The detail of a priest moonlighting as a food critic for a church newsletter is precisely the kind of specific, low-stakes eccentricity that makes great supporting character comedy. It's not a big joke but it's a reliable one — the incongruity is charming and characterizing.

jokes.text: 'I'd spend 40 days and 40 nights in that dessert.'
jokes.setup: The Priest has been coy about his restaurant review title. Fleabag and he have playfully established that neither of them is 'cool.' He finally reveals the title.
jokes.punchline: The pun on 'desert/dessert' — with a Biblical 40 days and 40 nights reference applied to a really good pudding — is charming, dorky, and perfectly in character for a bookish priest who takes mild pleasure in wordplay
jokes.explanation: This pun earns its high scores because it is character-specific in a way that a generic pun is not. Only THIS character — a priest who writes restaurant reviews and is 'a big reader with no friends' — would be proud of this particular joke. The setup (he won't say it; it's not cool; they've established neither of them is cool) makes the reveal land harder. And then Fleabag's reaction — 'Oh God, I fancy a priest' — confirms that the pun worked on her, which is the best possible endorsement. The pun itself is genuinely good: scripturally grounded, culinary, a real double meaning, and delivered with the shy pride of a man who has been waiting to tell someone. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Fleabag turns to camera: 'Oh God, I fancy a priest.'
jokes.setup: The Priest has just delivered his pun — 'I'd spend 40 days and 40 nights in that dessert'
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's direct-to-camera confession — horrified, delighted, helpless — is the perfect punctuation. The involuntary 'Oh God' as an opener adds another layer given the context.
jokes.explanation: One of the most iconic moments in the series. The joke operates on three levels simultaneously: (1) it's genuinely funny as a reaction to a priest's dorky pun; (2) 'Oh God' as an exclamation from an atheist falling for a priest is an involuntary theological comedy; (3) it's the show's central romantic premise crystallized into five words. The economy is absolute. The character integration is total — no other character on television could deliver this line in this context with this meaning. The rewatch bonus is enormous because on every subsequent viewing you know exactly what this moment means. Among the best jokes in the series.

jokes.text: 'Oh, it's Chatty Wednesday. If you buy something you have to have a chat with someone you don't know. Loneliness pays.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag asks why everyone is talking to each other at the cafe — it seems unnaturally social
jokes.punchline: 'Loneliness pays' is a dark, pithy, perfectly cynical summation of a scheme that monetizes social isolation
jokes.explanation: 'Loneliness pays' is a two-word joke that functions as both a business model and a social critique. The economy is near-perfect. It's not character-specific (which limits the score) but as an observational dark joke it punches above its weight. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Fleabag is forced to talk to a stranger ('Where are you from?') because she hasn't bought anything — 'Chatty Wednesday' rules applied to her involuntarily
jokes.setup: Fleabag is trying to have a private conversation with her sister and is suddenly accosted by a stranger following the Chatty Wednesday rules
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's increasingly desperate refusals — 'I'm not part of this,' 'I shouldn't have to' — before capitulating with 'Tooting' is a perfect cringe escalation
jokes.explanation: Solid cringe comedy. The joke is that an awkward social mechanism corners Fleabag at exactly the wrong moment — and the embarrassed, indignant 'Tooting' surrender is a good button. Not exceptional but competent and amusing.

jokes.text: 'She hasn't bought anything yet, Joe.' Fleabag: 'Oh, shit! I'm so sorry.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has been forced into a Chatty Wednesday conversation she didn't want
jokes.punchline: The reveal that she's been caught breaking the rules by chatting without buying — and the shop employee publicly calls her out — escalates the absurd social trap
jokes.explanation: Functional escalation of the Chatty Wednesday joke. Adds to the absurdist social-rule comedy. Reliable rather than remarkable.

jokes.text: Random person at the shop compliments Fleabag's trainers mid-emotional-crisis. 'They're some pretty funky trainers.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag and Claire are having a tense, loaded conversation about a miscarriage
jokes.punchline: A complete stranger interrupts with an oblivious compliment about trainers — the intrusion of mundane social reality into genuine emotional distress
jokes.explanation: A Chatty Wednesday consequence joke — the forced socialisation intruding at the worst possible moment. Works as situational comedy of bad timing. The word 'funky' does some work. Not especially crafted but lands cleanly.

jokes.text: Stepmother saying she has six friends who had miscarriages — 'A lie' (Fleabag aside to camera) — and five never had children, 'but the sixth one did, and rather regretted it.'
jokes.setup: Stepmother is offering ostensible comfort to Claire (or Fleabag) about miscarriage, citing her friends' experiences
jokes.punchline: First punchline: Fleabag clocks 'A lie' for the camera (the friend count is made up). Second punchline: the sixth friend who had a child 'rather regretted it' — devastatingly bleak and delivered as comfort
jokes.explanation: Two distinct jokes in rapid succession. The 'A lie' aside is quick and devastating — Fleabag knows the Stepmother is performing empathy rather than expressing it. The second joke — the sixth friend who regretted having the child — is the darker and better one. It's offered as reassurance, which makes it funnier and darker simultaneously. Character-specific to the Stepmother, who consistently delivers devastating truths while meaning well (or not meaning well at all). Strong craft.

jokes.text: 'But the sixth one did, and rather regretted it.'
jokes.setup: She's just said 5 of 6 friends never had children; this is the follow-up about the one who did
jokes.punchline: '...and rather regretted it' — the one 'successful' outcome is presented as worse than the others. The escalation takes a cruel joke and makes it crueler.
jokes.explanation: This is the killer escalation beat of the Godmother's speech. The structure is: setup (5/6 never had kids, terrible), escalation ('but the sixth one did' — hope), punchline ('and rather regretted it' — hope crushed). The economy is superb: 'rather regretted it' uses the most British understatement possible for 'having a child was a mistake.' The character integration is excellent — only this particular villain of studied obliviousness could deliver this. Justifies 8.2.

jokes.text: 'Oh, I'm still thinking about it.' (when asked if she ever wanted children)
jokes.setup: The question 'Did you never want them [children]?' is posed to the Stepmother — she is clearly past childbearing age
jokes.punchline: 'Oh, I'm still thinking about it' — delivered without irony as if it's a perfectly live question — is a brilliant piece of deadpan denial of reality
jokes.explanation: The Stepmother's refusal to close the question of children is delusional, vain, and deeply funny — and completely in character. She is a woman who will not accept limitations. 'Still thinking about it' implies active consideration of something that is almost certainly no longer biologically possible, and she says it as though it's a perfectly reasonable response.

jokes.text: Fleabag texts her father; Stepmother has Dad's phone ('Oh, I saw that.')/ 'Oh, so he is here?' / 'Sorry, no. I've got his phone today.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag asks if her Dad is at the event after texting him with no response
jokes.punchline: The Stepmother has her husband's phone — she's monitoring his communications — delivered with total breezy nonchalance as though this is completely normal
jokes.explanation: A solid character detail that is both funny and slightly sinister — the Stepmother controls her husband's phone without comment or shame. The deadpan delivery makes it funnier than any acknowledgment would. Not exceptional but effective.

jokes.text: Stepmother says she had an orgasm finishing a painting — 'Well, let's hope we all get as much pleasure out of this one.'
jokes.setup: Stepmother is discussing one of her paintings, casually mentioning she orgasmed when she finished it
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's dry response ('Let's hope we all get as much pleasure out of this one') treats the overshare with complete composure while being subtly appalled
jokes.explanation: The overshare is very much the Stepmother's register — she combines artfulness with complete obliviousness to social norms. Fleabag's comeback is well-constructed: it responds directly, escalates with faux-politeness, and has the correct amount of bite. Both halves work.

jokes.text: Claire/Fleabag/Stepmother: 'Well, let's hope we all get as much pleasure out of this one.'
jokes.setup: The Stepmother has announced the orgasm. A beat of silence. Someone needs to respond.
jokes.punchline: Claire's response is perfectly diplomatic and deeply sarcastic — 'let's hope we all get as much pleasure' — acknowledging the insane thing that was said while maintaining social function
jokes.explanation: A good comedic response to the orgasm revelation. Very Claire — she acknowledges the absurdity with plausibly deniable politeness. The double meaning of 'pleasure' (she's being literal/ironic) is well-handled. Solid professional comedy writing.

jokes.text: 'Gosh, haven't you got a lovely, thick neck.'
jokes.setup: The Stepmother has been directing a photo session. She now notices something about Claire or Fleabag physically.
jokes.punchline: Complimenting a neck as 'lovely and thick' is technically a compliment that sounds like an insult — the Stepmother seems unaware it's not flattering, which is entirely in character
jokes.explanation: A concise, perfectly aimed cringe joke. The Stepmother's sincere admiration of a 'lovely, thick neck' inverts the compliment form without her noticing. It's character-specific — only this woman, with her peculiar aesthetic sensibility, would say this — and the economy is excellent. The joke is the non-apology of an oblivious person being accidentally devastating.

jokes.text: 'Oh, he's a friend. He mainly defends rapists.' / 'He has a high success rate, then?'
jokes.setup: Claire is arranging a lawyer to scare off Martin; Fleabag asks who the lawyer is
jokes.punchline: Claire's casual 'He mainly defends rapists' — offered as a credential — is dark enough. Fleabag's 'He has a high success rate, then?' transforms the darkness into a perfectly structured joke about institutional failure around sexual violence
jokes.explanation: This exchange is sharper than it first appears. Claire offers 'defends rapists' as a quasi-recommendation, and Fleabag's comeback — 'high success rate, then?' — is a bleak, economical observation about rape conviction rates disguised as a quick comeback. The joke works as comedy AND social commentary, which is rare and valuable. Both characters' voices are distinct and recognizable. High economy score because the joke does a lot of work very fast.

jokes.text: David the lawyer: 'I just want to be clear that whatever happens, I don't sleep with people I work with, OK?' — immediately followed by 'I'm joking.'
jokes.setup: Claire has brought Fleabag to meet David, a handsome lawyer, insisting she not flirt with him
jokes.punchline: David preemptively announces he doesn't sleep with clients — seeming to be appropriately professional — then immediately undercuts it with 'I'm joking,' revealing he is exactly the kind of man Claire warned about
jokes.explanation: A reliable misdirection structure executed cleanly. The 'I'm joking' reveal works because it confirms Claire's warning was justified while pretending otherwise. Not exceptional but functional and character-establishing.

jokes.text: Fleabag's aside to camera: 'They've definitely fucked.' / 'They haven't.' / 'Oh, God, I've got to get out.'
jokes.setup: There is obvious tension and subtext between Claire and David during the legal meeting
jokes.punchline: Fleabag conducts an internal debate with herself via camera asides — 'They've definitely fucked' / 'They haven't' — before arriving at 'I've got to get out' as her conclusion
jokes.explanation: The use of the camera asides to conduct a running analysis of her sister's possible affair is both funny and reveals how Fleabag processes the world — as an observer commentating. The rapid-fire self-correction ('They've definitely fucked' / 'They haven't') followed by the exit decision is a strong structure. Character integration is high.

jokes.text: David: 'The most important thing, honey, is that you do not under any circumstances apologise.' Claire: 'I can do that.' David: 'No, that's not what we discussed.'
jokes.setup: David is giving legal advice: don't apologise. Seems simple.
jokes.punchline: Claire immediately agrees too enthusiastically — she'd LOVE to not apologise — but David corrects her: he told Claire not Fleabag, and Claire's eagerness reveals she applies the 'don't apologise' rule to her whole life
jokes.explanation: Strong character comedy. The joke reveals that Claire's reflex was to claim the advice for herself — she too refuses to apologise, and not just legally. David's correction ('that's not what we discussed') is the perfect deadpan response to a very Claire-shaped misunderstanding. High character integration.

jokes.text: Claire reveals she's decided not to tell Martin about the miscarriage and has been keeping him in the dark; then: 'I don't do that anymore.' / 'What? Why? Are you ill?'
jokes.setup: Fleabag says 'I don't do that anymore' (referring to sleeping with people casually). Claire — who knows Fleabag's history — cannot process this claim.
jokes.punchline: 'Why? Are you ill?' — Claire's only logical explanation for Fleabag's sexual abstinence is illness, which is both a character judgment and a sisterly joke. Then her follow-up interrogation escalates perfectly.
jokes.explanation: Claire's 'Are you ill?' is a beautifully sisterly diagnosis — it's genuinely funny because it reveals exactly how she categorizes Fleabag's behavior, and it comes from a place of authentic knowledge rather than judgment for its own sake. The subsequent interrogation ('Back with Harry?' / 'Someone new?' / 'Is he single?') escalating in speed is well-paced.

jokes.text: Claire, about the lawyer: 'He's a very good lawyer. Surprisingly... tender underneath it all.' Fleabag's camera look: 'Knew it.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag had clocked ('they're going to') that Claire and David have chemistry
jokes.punchline: 'Surprisingly tender underneath it all' is Claire revealing more than she intends; Fleabag's camera side-eye 'Knew it' is the confirmation of her earlier prediction
jokes.explanation: Good callback structure. Claire's 'tender underneath it all' is involuntary confession, Fleabag's 'Knew it' closes the loop on her earlier prediction. Works well as a two-beat that pays off the earlier observation. Solid.

jokes.text: 'Is he single?' / 'Sort of.'
jokes.setup: Claire is pressing Fleabag about who she has feelings for — 'Is he single?'
jokes.punchline: 'Sort of' — referring to a Catholic priest who is technically 'married to God' — is a devastatingly funny understatement of his availability status
jokes.explanation: Two words carrying enormous comedic freight. On first watch, 'Sort of' seems like a normal hedging answer. On reflection — and particularly on rewatch — it's a precise joke about celibacy, priesthood, and the nature of divine commitment. The economy is near-perfect. The character integration is high because this is exactly how Fleabag would answer — honest but evasive. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Fleabag's aside about the therapy voucher: 'I'd rather have the money.'
jokes.setup: Her father has given Fleabag a therapy voucher as a birthday present
jokes.punchline: 'I'd rather have the money' — said to camera rather than to the giver, revealing that she knows exactly what she needs (money) and dismisses the therapeutic gesture entirely
jokes.explanation: A clean, economical joke that doubles as character insight — Fleabag's resistance to being 'fixed' and her practical prioritization of cash over emotional care. Not exceptional but solidly constructed.

jokes.text: Therapy session: 'It would be good not to make jokes in here just in case anything gets lost in humorous translation.' / 'Oh, I don't know if I can do that.'
jokes.setup: The therapist is setting ground rules for the session
jokes.punchline: Fleabag immediately reveals she cannot NOT make jokes — and her honest admission is itself a joke, which is immediately pointed out by the therapist's 'Is that a joke?'
jokes.explanation: A meta joke about the show's own mechanism. Fleabag cannot exist without humor — it is how she survives, how she communicates, how she avoids genuine contact. The therapist telling her not to make jokes, and Fleabag immediately making one, and the therapist asking 'Is that a joke?' is a perfect structural loop. It's also the most honest self-diagnosis in the episode: she literally cannot comply with the request to be unguarded. Character integration is exceptional — this is the show commenting on itself. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Fleabag's therapy monologue: 'I think because my mother died and he can't talk about it / and my sister and I didn't speak for a year because she thinks I tried to sleep with her husband / and because I spent most of my adult life using sex to deflect from the screaming void inside my empty heart. I'm good at this.'
jokes.setup: The therapist asks why Fleabag's father suggested counselling — expecting a modest answer
jokes.punchline: The cascade of increasingly heavy admissions ending with 'I'm good at this' — cheerfully, as if proud of her self-diagnosis — is a masterclass in tonal whiplash. The 'screaming void inside my empty heart' is both completely sincere and delivered at speed to minimize its impact.
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional writing. The joke builds through escalation — dead mother, estranged sister, sexual compulsion — and lands with 'I'm good at this,' which is simultaneously sad and funny and true. Fleabag is good at listing her trauma because she has had a lot of practice using articulation as deflection. The 'screaming void inside my empty heart' is the series thesis statement delivered at comic speed so it doesn't hurt as much. 'I'm good at this' is the punchline that acknowledges the performance while being the performance. One of the finest moments in the episode. Justifies high craft score.

jokes.text: 'Any friends?' (long pause) 'Any friends?' / '...no, I don't really have time for... Well, I have a guinea pig, but she blows hot and cold. Not a joke.'
jokes.setup: The therapist asks if Fleabag has friends — Fleabag stalls with the classic 'I don't have time' excuse before admitting to a guinea pig
jokes.punchline: 'She blows hot and cold' — the guinea pig as an emotionally inconsistent friend is absurd. 'Not a joke' is the button — but of course it is a joke AND is also literally true, which is the Fleabag mode entirely
jokes.explanation: 'She blows hot and cold' applied to a guinea pig is a specific, absurd, and darkly revealing joke. The fact that Fleabag characterizes her pet's affection as inconsistent tells us everything about her attachment style. 'Not a joke' is the crucial addition — it asserts the reality of the loneliness while also being funny about it, which is exactly Fleabag's register. Strong craft marks throughout. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Therapist: 'Tell me about the sex.' Fleabag: 'All of it?'
jokes.setup: Standard therapeutic follow-up after Fleabag mentions using sex to deflect emotional void
jokes.punchline: 'All of it?' — the implication is that there is a genuinely enormous quantity, and Fleabag's concern is logistical rather than emotional
jokes.explanation: Earns 8.0. Three words that do extraordinary character work. The question 'All of it?' implies volume (funny), deflection through logistics (character-true), and a kind of proud ambiguity. Maximum funny per word. The Therapist has no good response.

jokes.text: 'I just play tennis now.' (in response to 'You said you don't do that now' about sex)
jokes.setup: The therapist asks what she has replaced sex with, as a substitute for using it as deflection
jokes.punchline: 'I just play tennis now' — offered as a completely straight, functional answer, with zero acknowledgment of how inadequate tennis is as a substitute for sex
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the specificity and nonchalance of the substitution. 'Tennis' is exactly the right choice — respectable, energetic, a bit posh, vaguely British, and obviously insufficient. The delivery is straight-faced, which is everything. Good economy.

jokes.text: Therapist says 'Tough crowd.' after Fleabag's tennis joke gets no laugh from the therapist
jokes.setup: Fleabag has made a joke (the tennis line); the therapist doesn't laugh
jokes.punchline: 'Tough crowd' — the therapist using stand-up comedy language to comment on her own blank response is self-aware in a way that briefly turns the table
jokes.explanation: The therapist adopting the performer's lament in a clinical setting is a small but effective meta moment — it briefly makes her the comedian and Fleabag the audience. It also warms the therapist's character. Solid.

jokes.text: 'Well, I'm very horny, and your little scarf isn't helping.'
jokes.setup: The therapist asks what Fleabag has found in her period of sexual abstinence — expecting some positive discovery
jokes.punchline: Fleabag confesses to being chronically horny and specifically names the therapist's scarf as an arousal trigger — a complete overshare that turns the therapist into an unwitting participant in her desire
jokes.explanation: The specificity of the scarf is the joke's engine. It would be less funny if Fleabag said 'I find you attractive' — but 'your little scarf isn't helping' is hilarious because it's so specific, so absurd, and so honest. It's a perfectly Fleabag overshare: she says the exact wrong true thing at the exact wrong moment. Character integration is high.

jokes.text: Fleabag describes the man she's not having sex with: 'He's not available.' / 'In a relationship?' / 'Yes. A bad one.' / 'How so?' / 'It's the sort of relationship where one partner tells the other how to dress.'
jokes.setup: The therapist is pressing Fleabag on who the unavailable man is. Fleabag describes his relationship problem.
jokes.punchline: 'It's the sort of relationship where one partner tells the other how to dress' — referring to a priest being told what to wear by the Catholic Church, which she delivers as if it were a controlling girlfriend situation. The misdirection is perfect.
jokes.explanation: This is superb misdirection comedy. On first hearing, 'one partner tells the other how to dress' sounds like a controlling domestic relationship. The reveal — that it's God/the Church dictating clerical dress — is funnier the more you sit with it. It also characterizes the priest's vocation as a relationship with a very demanding, very specific partner. The joke works emotionally (she's making fun of her own impossible situation) and intellectually (the metaphor is precise and original). Justifies 8+ strongly.

jokes.text: Therapist: 'Are you in love with him?' Fleabag: 'No.' Therapist: 'Why do you find that funny?' Fleabag: 'Well, I... I lied. I just... I don't know.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has denied being in love
jokes.punchline: The Therapist catches her laughing at her own 'No' — a tell — and Fleabag's defences crumble into 'I lied' almost immediately. The joke is the speed of her capitulation.
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+. The comedy here is in the structural collapse: Fleabag's defences fail in about three seconds. 'I lied' delivered with resigned bewilderment rather than shame is the perfect Fleabag mode — self-aware and defeated simultaneously. The Therapist's observation ('why do you find that funny?') is the sharp knife. Very well-played character moment.

jokes.text: The therapy session: Fleabag talks about 'someone' who is 'always there' — while we understand she means the audience she speaks to directly. 'Do you see them a lot?' / 'They're always there.'
jokes.setup: The therapist has asked if Fleabag has friends. Fleabag says yes. The therapist asks who.
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's description of her 'friends' — 'they're always there' (said twice, with increasing private meaning) — is about the audience. She is talking about us. This is the show's central meta conceit made explicit in therapy, which is both funny and deeply moving.
jokes.explanation: This is the most sophisticated joke in the episode and arguably the most sophisticated structural joke in the series. The fourth wall is not just being broken — it is being examined in a therapy session. Fleabag is talking to a therapist about the audience, her invisible constant companions, while never explaining what she means. The therapist's earnest 'Do you see them a lot?' / 'Oh, so you do have someone to talk to' is both comedic (because she has no idea) and devastating (because the audience IS Fleabag's only emotional outlet). The laughter she suppresses when asked 'Why do you find that funny?' is the show acknowledging its own machinery. Exceptional in originality, character integration, structure, and emotional impact. Among the finest moments in Fleabag.

jokes.text: Therapist: 'Just a girl with no friends and an empty heart?' Fleabag: 'By your own description.' Pause. Fleabag: 'I have friends.'
jokes.setup: Therapist uses Fleabag's own earlier self-description back at her
jokes.punchline: Fleabag defends herself against her own words, then immediately qualifies with a hollow 'I have friends' — which we know means either the camera audience (us) or Boo (dead)
jokes.explanation: Earns 8.5. 'I have friends' is devastating. It's funny as a defensive pivot; it's tragic because her 'friends' are a dead woman and a TV audience; and the subsequent conversation where she implies 'they're always there' with a camera-glance confirms that the friend is us. This is the show's formal conceit used at its most emotionally complex. Among the episode's best moments.

jokes.text: 'I want the money.' (said to camera, after being told it's too late to exchange the voucher)
jokes.setup: The therapist has said it's too late to exchange the voucher for money. Fleabag accepts this verbally.
jokes.punchline: Fleabag turns to camera and says simply 'I want the money.' — refusing to process the emotional work on offer and reverting to pure financial pragmatism
jokes.explanation: A callback that deepens the original joke. The persistent return to 'the money' over emotional healing is both funny and sad — she would genuinely rather have cash than sit with her feelings, which is the character's core defense mechanism made visible.

jokes.text: 'I want to fuck a priest.' — Fleabag's confession to the therapist
jokes.setup: The therapy session has been probing Fleabag's abstinence and her mysterious unavailable man. She has been dancing around it.
jokes.punchline: The flat, unadorned declaration 'I want to fuck a priest' as the distillation of the entire episode's romantic tension is both funny and shocking in a therapy context — Fleabag presenting the most charged, taboo desire of the episode as a clinical statement of fact
jokes.explanation: A callback that escalates both the comedy and the honesty. Where 'Oh God, I fancy a priest' was a private confession to the audience, 'I want to fuck a priest' is a public declaration to a professional. The escalation in explicitness mirrors the escalation in Fleabag's self-awareness. The therapist's follow-up ('Do you want to fuck the priest or do you want to fuck God?') makes this even funnier and more destabilizing. Economy is exceptional — six words, maximum information.

jokes.text: 'Do you want to fuck the priest or do you want to fuck God?' / 'Can you fuck God?' / 'Oh, yes.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has confessed to wanting to sleep with a priest. The therapist responds with a theological-psychological question.
jokes.punchline: The therapist's confident 'Oh, yes' — confirming that yes, people do (metaphorically) fuck God, and this is a recognized therapeutic concept — is delivered with complete professional calm, which is the joke
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'fuck the priest' to 'fuck God' is a genuine theological-comedic move. The therapist is raising the stakes — is this desire about the man or about what he represents? And then 'Can you fuck God?' / 'Oh, yes' creates an absurdist exchange where spiritual transcendence and sexual desire become indistinguishable. The confidence of 'Oh, yes' is everything — no hedging, no explanation, total professional certainty about a preposterous question. Strong marks across the board. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'You already know what you're going to do. Everybody does.' / 'What's the point in you?'
jokes.setup: The therapist tells Fleabag she already knows what she's going to do (pursue the priest)
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's 'What's the point in you?' — a genuine indictment of therapy delivered to the therapist's face — is both funny and a genuine philosophical challenge to the therapeutic enterprise
jokes.explanation: 'What's the point in you?' is one of the best lines in the episode. It dismantles the therapeutic frame by accepting its logic (you already know what you'll do) and then following it to its conclusion (so why am I here?). It's genuinely funny, genuinely philosophical, and completely Fleabag — she cannot resist the logical takedown even when she's the one who needs help. Justifies 8+ for character integration and economy.

jokes.text: 'You know what you're going to do.' / 'No, I don't.' / 'Yes, you do.' / 'I don't.' / 'You do. You do.' / 'I don't. I don't!' / 'Good luck.' / 'Thank you.' / 'Shut up.'
jokes.setup: The therapist and Fleabag are locked in a standoff about whether Fleabag knows what she's going to do
jokes.punchline: The exchange escalates in childish intensity — 'I don't! I don't!' — before ending with 'Good luck' / 'Thank you' / 'Shut up.' The final 'Shut up' punctuates it perfectly.
jokes.explanation: The Yes/No escalation is a classic structure, but it's justified here by the content — Fleabag's insistence that she doesn't know what she'll do is itself the behavior the therapist is diagnosing. The 'Shut up' button is well-placed. The professional relationship dissolves into a mutual acknowledgment that works like a comedic grace note.

jokes.text: Fleabag bumps into Suzie — her ex-girlfriend — who is visibly very pregnant at the fete
jokes.setup: Fleabag is buying raffle tickets and then suddenly recognizes someone
jokes.punchline: 'You're... Yeah. Yes. With child. Cool.' — Fleabag's hiccupping acknowledgment of Suzie's pregnancy, processing each element separately
jokes.explanation: 'With child. Cool.' has the characteristic Fleabag stammer-and-recover, but the setup here is fairly standard sitcom territory (unexpected ex-encounter). Functional comedy.

jokes.text: 'You got a fringe.' / 'Yeah.' / 'Yeah. Cool.' / 'Aw. You always wanted a baby.' / 'You always wanted a fringe.'
jokes.setup: Two exes note each other's biggest changes since they split — one has a baby, one has a fringe
jokes.punchline: The parallel structure — 'You always wanted a baby / You always wanted a fringe' — places a hairstyle alongside a child as equivalent life achievements, which is both funny and a perfect encapsulation of their respective situations
jokes.explanation: The parallel construction is elegant. 'You always wanted a baby' / 'You always wanted a fringe' places two completely disparate life events in grammatical equivalence — which is both absurd and affectionate. It's a way for both characters to be kind to each other about their respective achievements without diminishing either. The economy is excellent. Justifies 8+ for structure and economy.

jokes.text: The ex-husband/partner announces he has postnatal depression — 'I do, yeah, but we're working through it.'
jokes.setup: Suzie's partner has been describing his emotional difficulties since the birth — mood fluctuations, changed body — and Fleabag bluntly diagnoses 'Sounds like you have postnatal depression'
jokes.punchline: He confirms it ('I do, yeah') — the punchline being that a man has postnatal depression, takes it completely seriously, and accepts the diagnosis without defensiveness
jokes.explanation: A modern, gently subversive joke — the punchline isn't that he has it but that he openly acknowledges it ('we're working through it') in the most mundane possible way. The humor comes from the normalization. Solid, contemporary comedy.

jokes.text: 'Oh, I don't know how to talk to babies.' (Priest, when introduced to Suzie's baby)
jokes.setup: Fleabag introduces the Priest to Suzie. Suzie has her baby.
jokes.punchline: The Priest apologizes to the baby for not knowing how to talk to it — with complete honesty and no embarrassment — which is somehow both unusual and perfectly him
jokes.explanation: The Priest's honest admission about babies is character-specific — a man who performs pastoral care for everyone but genuinely doesn't have the social software for infants. The humor is in the honesty and the specificity.

jokes.text: 'I used to be her girlfriend.' — Suzie explaining her relationship with Fleabag to the Priest
jokes.setup: The Priest asks how Suzie and Fleabag know each other, right after Fleabag was about to explain
jokes.punchline: 'I used to be her girlfriend' — dropped casually as information, right in front of a Catholic priest, who responds with 'Oh, cool. Good for you.'
jokes.explanation: The joke is the Priest's casual, un-scandalized response — 'Oh, cool. Good for you.' — which characterizes him as a progressive and comfortable man in a context where a conservative reaction might be expected. Functional.

jokes.text: Priest announces 'The Youthy Band is about to play the ode to something!' with complete uncertainty about the title
jokes.setup: Pam is trying to get the Priest to announce the band; he finally does
jokes.punchline: 'The ode to something!' — he has no idea what they're playing. The vagueness is perfect: 'something' instead of any specific title implies complete disengagement from the details of his own event.
jokes.explanation: 'The ode to something' is a quietly funny bit of incompetent event management. Charming rather than hilarious.

jokes.text: Fleabag tells the Priest about the painting — 'she actually orgasmed when she finished it' — immediately after saying 'Just don't say it'
jokes.setup: The Stepmother's orgasm-during-painting detail has already been a joke. Fleabag is clearly trying not to repeat it to the Priest.
jokes.punchline: 'Just said it. Apparently.' — Fleabag turns to camera having failed to contain the overshare, delivering her own self-flagellation
jokes.explanation: A good callback that adds the self-aware layer — Fleabag narrating her own failure to contain the inappropriate detail while committing the exact failure she was trying to avoid. 'Just said it. Apparently.' is a beautifully self-mocking aside.

jokes.text: Priest responds to the painting orgasm information with: 'Well, whatever gets you there.'
jokes.setup: The orgasm-painting revelation lands. Someone must respond.
jokes.punchline: 'Whatever gets you there' — pastoral tolerance applied to sexual creativity. The Priest accepts this with the equanimity of a man who has heard everything in confession
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment. 'Whatever gets you there' is the confessional priest's tolerance principle applied with perfect economy. It implies a depth of experience with human weirdness that the Priest carries lightly. Very character-specific.

jokes.text: Pam: 'Father, we have a cupcake situation over here!' / Priest: 'OK, Pam! I will be there to cupcake!'
jokes.setup: The Priest and Fleabag are having a moment of connection. Pam interrupts.
jokes.punchline: 'I will be there to cupcake' — the Priest uses 'cupcake' as a verb, which is both a malapropism and an earnest attempt to respond in Pam's register
jokes.explanation: 'I will be there to cupcake' is a delightful verbal tic — the Priest matching Pam's slightly chaotic energy by turning a noun into a verb. It's character-specific and charming.

jokes.text: Fleabag aside to camera: 'Arm touch.'
jokes.setup: The Priest and Fleabag have a moment of physical proximity — he touches her arm as he leaves
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's single-word/two-word aside — 'Arm touch' — reports the contact to the audience with the breathless exactitude of a sports commentator calling a significant play
jokes.explanation: Two words. The joke is perfect. 'Arm touch' functions as both comedy and love story — Fleabag is so overwhelmed by the priest's incidental arm contact that she must immediately report it to her only confidants (us). The deadpan reportage of something so minor as if it were seismic is both funny and heartbreaking. The economy is absolute — she cannot say more; this is all she has. It is one of the most economical jokes in the series and one of the most emotionally resonant. Justifies 9 strongly.

jokes.text: Martin: 'At least my son is in the Youthy Band. What's your excuse?' / Fleabag: 'I'm helping the priest.' / Martin: 'Wow, you do love a challenge, don't you?'
jokes.setup: Martin is being his usual provocative self at the fete. He challenges Fleabag about why she's there.
jokes.punchline: 'You do love a challenge, don't you?' — Martin is being snide, but accidentally summarizes the entire show's romantic premise (Fleabag pursuing a priest IS an impossible challenge) with one line
jokes.explanation: Martin's snide remark accidentally lands as an unwitting thematic statement. The joke works on two levels: surface snark and inadvertent truth. A villain being the one to articulate the series theme is satisfying and a little funny. Rewatch bonus because subsequent viewings make the layering more apparent.

jokes.text: Fleabag's stepbrother's speech dissolves into insult — 'I'm happy you've found a way to deflect from your pitiful, self-sabotaging, ego-driven, masturbatory... insidious, insidious, overwhelming mediocrity only to finally figure out that at your very core you are a... w-weaky.'
jokes.setup: A character appears to be making a heartfelt speech about Fleabag's resilience
jokes.punchline: The speech reveals itself to be an insult — a cascade of increasingly precise adjectives — before losing steam entirely and landing on 'weaky.' The deflation of 'weaky' after 'masturbatory' and 'insidious' is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The escalation joke works because the speech starts sounding like something else and then reveals the darkness underneath, before collapsing into the infantile 'weaky.' The anti-climactic punchline word — after such a vocabulary build — is the joke. 'Masturbatory' in a speech at a church fete has its own shock value. The 'Damn! A weaky?' self-critique afterward is a nice button.

jokes.text: 'You're a... strongy.' (as recovery from 'weaky')
jokes.setup: 'Weaky' has already collapsed the speech. The speaker tries to recover with a compliment.
jokes.punchline: 'You're a... strongy' — the invented word 'strongy' as the corrective after 'weaky' is the natural logical completion of an already broken vocabulary
jokes.explanation: The callback to 'weaky' with 'strongy' as its opposite completes the joke's grammar — if the insult collapses vocabulary, so does the compliment. 'Strongy' is funnier than anything more articulate would have been.

jokes.text: Creepy Jake description: 'Mainly says things like... Where's Claire? And... Where's Claire?'
jokes.setup: Fleabag introduces 'Creepy Jake' to the audience via camera aside
jokes.punchline: Jake's entire characterization is two repetitions of 'Where's Claire?' — suggesting his entire personality is obsessive fixation, delivered by Fleabag with the efficiency of someone who has no time to explain further
jokes.explanation: A perfect miniature character comedy. The 'Mainly says things like... Where's Claire? And... Where's Claire?' construction creates a character portrait in eight words — the repetition IS the joke and the characterization simultaneously. Economical, precise, and very funny. Justifies 8+ for economy and character integration.

jokes.text: Martin's aggressive behavior toward a person giving an innocent hug: 'You could go down for that sort of behaviour these days... you got to do better than that these days, boy.'
jokes.setup: Someone gives what seems to be an innocent hug at the fete. Martin weaponizes contemporary sensitivity around physical contact.
jokes.punchline: Martin uses the language of #MeToo accountability to threaten and control — 'You could go down for that' — which is both a cringe joke about his behaviour and a dark observation about how concern for consent can be weaponized
jokes.explanation: This joke is darker than it plays — Martin using the language of accountability to assert dominance is unsettling and character-consistent, but the joke isn't quite constructed precisely enough to fully land as intended. It's more cringe-drama than pure comedy.

jokes.text: Creepy Jake whispers to Fleabag: 'Tell her to leave him. He's gonna kill someone one day.'
jokes.setup: Jake has sought out Fleabag privately for a covert message
jokes.punchline: 'He's gonna kill someone one day' — delivered as a matter-of-fact prediction rather than an alarm, with complete calm — is so extreme that it becomes darkly funny, especially from the character whose entire personality is 'Where's Claire?'
jokes.explanation: The joke works because Jake has been established as someone who only says 'Where's Claire?' — and now delivers an escalated, genuinely alarming prediction as if it's the most natural follow-up. The deadpan and the stakes mismatch creates dark humor.

jokes.text: 'Can I get that coconut back? They're actually on hire... I'm not sure if a lot of them are even real actually, which is morally a bit dubious. But we got to make money somehow.'
jokes.setup: The Priest returns to Fleabag with an embarrassing request — he needs the coconut back from the shy
jokes.punchline: His admission that the coconuts might be fake ('morally a bit dubious') followed immediately by 'But we got to make money somehow' is a perfectly calibrated self-aware hypocrisy — a priest acknowledging small-scale deceit with complete equanimity
jokes.explanation: The fake coconuts as a small moral compromise — acknowledged openly by the man in charge of moral instruction — is a lovely piece of character comedy. 'Morally a bit dubious' is a perfect understatement for fake coconuts at a church fete. Character integration is high.

jokes.text: 'Knuckle brush.' (Fleabag aside to camera after the Priest's hand grazes hers during the book exchange)
jokes.setup: The Priest hands Fleabag a marked-up book. Their hands make minimal contact.
jokes.punchline: 'Knuckle brush' — Fleabag reports the contact to the audience with the same sportscaster breathlessness as 'Arm touch,' establishing a running gag of cataloguing incidental physical proximity to the Priest
jokes.explanation: The callback to 'Arm touch' makes 'Knuckle brush' exponentially funnier. What was already a perfect joke becomes a running gag — a taxonomy of forbidden contact, each more minimal than the last. 'Knuckle brush' after 'Arm touch' suggests that even a grazing of knuckles is worth cataloguing and reporting. The economy is absolute — two words carry the entire weight of the episode's romantic tension, its comedy, and its sadness. This is the best running gag in the episode and among the most economical jokes in British television comedy. The rewatch bonus is enormous because it rewards everyone who recognized the pattern.

jokes.text: 'You can come whenever you want. I'd like you to come. If it helps.'
jokes.setup: The Priest is giving Fleabag his contact details and inviting her to visit. He is speaking entirely sincerely about pastoral availability.
jokes.punchline: The repeated use of 'come' in a context loaded with sexual tension — 'You can come whenever you want. I'd like you to come.' — is an unintentional double entendre that is funnier precisely because the Priest is completely unaware of it
jokes.explanation: The unintentional double entendre works because (a) it's clearly unintentional on the Priest's part, (b) Fleabag is fully aware of it, and (c) the episode has established exactly why this is charged. The joke earns its laughs not from the pun itself but from the specific context — we know what Fleabag wants, and the Priest is unknowingly delivering a line she cannot respond to with any version of honesty.

tmdb_overview: A counselling session elicits an uncomfortable truth from Fleabag, and she finds herself somewhere unexpected. Elsewhere, a chat with Claire brings some unwelcome news, Martin and Fleabag face off, and Jake wonders where Claire is.
